name: AppSignal APM
what_it_does: AppSignal is an APM and error monitoring platform purpose-built for Ruby, Elixir, and Node.js that collects errors, performance traces, custom metrics, and logs through a REST API and language-native agents.
use_cases: Querying recent error incidents for a specific app to triage production issues
Fetching performance metrics (throughput, response time, Apdex) for a time range to detect regressions
Pulling log streams for a deployment window to correlate errors with a release
Creating or updating alert triggers programmatically when deploying new services
Retrieving host-level metrics (CPU, memory, disk) to correlate infrastructure issues with application errors
not_for: Polyglot stacks — deep instrumentation is limited to Ruby, Elixir, and Node.js (other languages lack native agents)
Full-stack distributed tracing across many microservices (Datadog or Jaeger is more capable at that scale)
Real user monitoring (RUM) or front-end JavaScript performance tracking
best_when: The stack is primarily Ruby on Rails, Phoenix, or Node.js and the team wants a low-configuration, all-in-one APM with errors, traces, logs, and metrics in one place.
avoid_when: The application is written in Go, Java, Python, or another language not supported by AppSignal's native agents, or the team requires a self-hosted APM solution.

auth.notes: API authentication uses a personal API token passed as a query parameter (?token=) or Authorization header. App-level push API keys are used by language agents for data ingestion. No OAuth or fine-grained scopes.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free trial available; free tier limited to 1 app with limited retention and alerting
pricing.paid_tiers: Plans start around $18/month per app, scaling with host count and data retention
pricing.notes: Pricing is per app per month. Higher tiers unlock longer retention, more hosts, and advanced features like anomaly detection.

performance.rate_limits: Not publicly documented; practical limits apply to data ingestion endpoints

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API errors return JSON with a message field but HTTP status codes are the primary signal; some error responses lack actionable detail for automated retry logic.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read endpoints are idempotent. Write endpoints (markers, alerts) do not provide idempotency keys.
known_agent_gotchas: API token is passed as a query parameter in many examples, which risks token leakage in server logs
The REST API for reading data (errors, metrics) is separate and less documented than the data ingestion (push) API
App identifier (app_id) must be obtained through the dashboard before making most API calls — no discovery endpoint
Time ranges in queries use Unix timestamps and timezone handling is not clearly documented
Rate limit headers are not consistently returned, making adaptive throttling difficult for agents

security_score_components.security_notes: API token passed as query parameter is a security concern. No OAuth, no fine-grained scopes. TLS enforced on all endpoints.
